tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.details:
Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.
 - unknown: 6 occurrences<<list_path_1_call_1>>
hint.title: CALL instructions

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
 - DIV: 2 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_1>>
 - VZEROUPPER: 2 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_2>>

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 21.50 cycles. At this rate:
 - 1% of peak load performance is reached (2.98 out of 192.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
 - 17% of peak store performance is reached (22.51 out of 128.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.help: List of bottlenecks detected by CQA. A bottleneck is an issue which reduces front-end throughput below the core capacity.
expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks

header:
Warnings:
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	-0x2(%RDI),%R13D] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	(%R8,%RDX,1),%EAX] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	(%R9,%RAX,1),%R10D] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	-0x1(%R9),%R11D] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	0x110(%RSP),%RCX] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	0x2(%RDX),%R13D] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	0x2(%RAX),%R15D] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	0x68(%RSP),%RDI] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	0x60(%RSP),%R8] is unknown
 - The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA	-0x28(%RBP),%RSP] is unknown
 - Detected a function call instruction: ignoring called function instructions.
Rerun with --follow-calls=append to include them to analysis  or with --follow-calls=inline to simulate inlining.
0% of peak computational performance is used (0.00 out of 48.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
- Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
 - To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
 - For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this function
gain.title: Code clean check
gain.txt:
Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 21.50 to 6.00 cycles (3.58x speedup).

gain.workaround:
- Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
 - Make array accesses unit-stride:
  * If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)
gain.details:
28% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
 - 0% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
 - 38% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
 - 0% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
 - 0% of SSE/AVX multiply instructions are used in vector version.
 - 0% of SSE/AVX divide and square root instructions are used in vector version.
 - 21% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized function can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your function is poorly vectorized.
Only 35% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 21.50 to 14.54 cycles (1.48x speedup).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Performance is limited by writing data to caches/RAM (the store unit is a bottleneck).
